stories.title: Contributing to Structured Incident Response and Process Improvements During Billing System Outage
stories.summary: During my time as a Software Engineering Intern at Dropbox, I was directly involved in a major incident where the billing stack experienced about four hours of downtime, impacting payment processing and tier assignment for users. Although I wasn't leading the effort, I pair-programmed alongside a senior engineer, taking on keyboard duties for portions of the debugging loop, pulling logs and metrics, implementing test changes, and posting status updates that kept the team coordinated. Through tight, hypothesis-driven troubleshooting and concise communication, we managed to restore critical user functionality and contributed to a structured, blameless incident response process. This experience reinforced the value of calm, clear communication, systematic investigation, and optimizing for service restoration during high-pressure situations, lessons that have strongly shaped how I collaborate and respond to technical incidents in my career since.
stories.star_analysis:
During my internship as a Software Engineer at Dropbox, I found myself in the middle of a high-stakes emergency incident early in my career. The issue arose in the billing stack, which caused failure in payment processing as well as in the payment page itself. Most critically, users were unable to be properly assigned their correct service tier upon signing in, meaning many paying customers lost access to features they had purchased. The gravity of the situation set in quickly, and while I was nervous and under significant pressure, I knew the moment would be a key learning opportunity.

Our main task was clear and urgent: restore billing functionality and correct tier assignment as quickly as possible. We needed to identify the cause of the failure, assess the impact, and recover payment and entitlement processing so users could regain access to the services they expected. Simultaneously, we had to avoid missteps that could further exacerbate the outage or introduce instability elsewhere. As an intern, my personal responsibility was to assist the senior engineers by keeping the investigation moving efficiently and ensuring clear communication with the team.

My specific actions started with pairing closely with a senior engineer from the billing team. We quickly established a tight feedback loop: I took turns driving the keyboard, combing through logs, pulling key metrics, and helping identify consistent error signatures. Together, we formed and tested hypotheses about possible causes, prioritizing our investigation based on recent deploys and config changes. I posted concise, real-time updates in the incident channel to keep the team aligned on what we had ruled out and what steps we were taking next. Throughout, I focused on accelerating the debugging process—running quick checks, validating assumptions, and supporting logic-driven problem-solving—while respecting the guidance and decision-making of more experienced teammates.

The outcome was a successful and timely restoration of the billing stack: payment processing and the assignment of user entitlements were brought back online within around four hours. Beyond immediate recovery, our investigation clarified the underlying failure mode, which directly informed the post-incident review. This led to concrete improvements within the team: creation of better documentation (a runbook for future billing incidents), refinement of alerting and dashboards to catch similar problems earlier, and an updated approach to mitigation strategies with safer rollbacks. The structured, hypothesis-driven debugging loop we used that day became a model for subsequent incidents.

From this experience, I learned the importance of calm, structured communication and the value of pairing, especially for early-career engineers in stressful situations. I developed a habit of hypothesis-driven investigation, concise status reporting, and incremental validation—skills that I continue to leverage in later roles. I also internalized lessons in focusing on service restoration and collaboration over “heroics,” as reinforced by feedback from peers and mentors. This incident left a lasting mark on my approach to troubleshooting, teamwork, and high-pressure problem solving, and cemented key foundational habits for my career going forward.

stories.title: Launching a Conversational Search Interface to Boost User Adoption and Align Stakeholders
stories.summary:
During my time as a Product Manager at Zo Computer, my most impactful accomplishment was leading the redesign and launch of our file search feature. When I joined, we had a technically sophisticated but hardly used search backend—users found the interface unintuitive and struggled to accomplish simple tasks. I initiated and conducted extensive user research, which made it clear that people needed a natural, conversational way to search, not complex query syntax. Over the course of four months, I partnered with a small cross-functional team—one designer and four engineers—to prototype, validate, and implement a new natural language search interface, coordinating closely with engineering, customer success, sales, executive leadership, and external AI partners.

The launch resulted in a 40% increase in daily active users and a significant drop in support tickets. Navigating this project required me to manage diverse stakeholder perspectives, drive alignment using real user data, and learn the importance of early cost modeling for external APIs. This experience sharpened my skills in user research, cross-team collaboration, stakeholder management, and data-driven decision-making, and fundamentally changed my approach to building products and communicating with engineering.
stories.star_analysis:
Paragraph 1 (Situation): When I joined Zo Computer as a Product Manager, I inherited a search feature that was technically impressive but woefully underutilized. The engineering team had developed a sophisticated backend supporting complex queries, boolean operators, and regex patterns, but user engagement metrics were abysmal. Despite the engineers' pride in their work, users appeared to struggle with the system's complexity, and support tickets about search frustrations remained high. As a B2B company serving around 200 customer organizations with tens of thousands of end users, the usability of core features like file search was critical for overall customer satisfaction and product adoption.

Paragraph 2 (Task): My primary challenge was to transform the file search experience so that users could efficiently find the information they needed without advanced technical knowledge. The product needed to work intuitively for everyone, reducing support burdens while increasing usage and satisfaction. There was also a core alignment issue to resolve: engineering valued technical power and precision, while users, customer success, and sales teams all clamored for a simpler, more accessible search experience. I needed to devise a solution that balanced these competing priorities and achieved buy-in across a broad stakeholder group, from engineers to executives.

Paragraph 3 (Action): To tackle the problem, I began by conducting in-depth user research sessions to observe firsthand how real people interacted with the search feature. I synthesized these sessions into clear actionable insights, illustrating that users naturally searched by asking questions, not constructing complex queries. Partnering with our designer, we prototyped a conversational, natural language interface, tested it with users, and iteratively improved the design based on feedback. Internally, I bridged the divide between teams by sharing data-rich videos of struggling users and direct testing results, which shifted perspectives—particularly within engineering. I also coordinated with Customer Success to select beta participants and prepare launch materials, worked with our AI vendor to optimize natural language processing while modeling cost impacts for Finance, and regularly updated the CEO to ensure executive support.

Paragraph 4 (Results): The revamped feature was prototyped and built over four months with a focused team of four engineers (plus myself and our designer) and rolled out in a bold, all-at-once launch to approximately 50,000 end users. The daily active usage of search increased by 40% in the first quarter post-launch. Support tickets for search-related issues noticeably dropped, indicating real improvements in user satisfaction and accessibility. Advanced features were retained in an opt-in mode for power users, maintaining goodwill with technical stakeholders. The initiative also provided a potent new selling point for the Sales team and strengthened cross-functional collaboration within the company.

Paragraph 5 (Takeaways): From this project, I learned the value of honesty and evidence in stakeholder management—being direct about user needs, even when it challenged someone's work, led to more productive dialogue. Facilitating first-hand exposure to user struggles for team members proved more effective than relying solely on secondhand reports. I also realized the importance of anticipating technical and financial implications earlier in the process; neglecting early cost modeling for the AI API nearly caused a post-launch crisis, prompting me to proactively build financial projections into future projects. Overall, I strengthened my ability to lead cross-functional teams, ground discussions in real user data, and drive meaningful product improvements that balance technical capability with true usability.

stories.title: Rapid Crisis Response: Restoring Trust Through Minimal Viable Pricing Transparency
stories.summary: In my role as a Product Manager at Zo Computer, I led the response to a critical crisis stemming from a failed rollout of our dynamic AI-powered pricing feature. During a chaotic, high-pressure three-week period, I was responsible for diagnosing the issue by analyzing query logs and conducting direct calls with customers to understand their frustrations about unpredictable pricing. While coordinating closely with engineering, design, and customer success, I contributed beyond typical PM duties by writing frontend code and personally engaging with key clients, including one of our largest customers whose renewal was at risk. The solution—a real-time price preview—was designed and implemented rapidly, resulting in immediate customer relief, saved over $500K in potential lost revenue, and restored trust in our product. This experience reinforced my belief in prioritizing speed and clarity over perfection in urgent situations, and it highlighted the importance of direct customer engagement when resolving product crises.
stories.star_analysis:
1. Situation: About six months ago, shortly after we launched a new AI-powered feature at Zo Computer, our small startup faced a major challenge that placed both revenue and customer trust at risk. As a full-time product manager in a 40-person team, I was one of three PMs, and often found myself wearing many hats. The crisis erupted when our dynamic pricing engine, designed to charge customers based on compute usage for analytics queries, resulted in wildly inconsistent pricing. Customers saw unpredictable charges for seemingly identical queries, leading to a sudden spike in support tickets and vocal dissatisfaction from key accounts like Brightside.

2. Task: The immediate challenge was to restore customer trust and stabilize revenue in the face of this unpredictable, confusing pricing system. Brightside, our largest customer with 500 employees and contributing about $200K in annual revenue, threatened to walk away just weeks ahead of their renewal. In total, around $500K in annual recurring revenue was at risk, as other customers were also considering leaving and prospective deals stalled. My task was to identify the root cause of the pricing confusion, quickly design and deliver a fix that would stop customer churn, and regain lost trust before irreversible damage was done.

3. Action: My first move was to analyze query logs to see whether the pricing spikes were as random as they appeared. Realizing raw data alone wouldn’t tell the full story, I got on live calls with Marcus from Customer Success and several affected customers, including screen-share sessions that revealed their deep confusion. This process swiftly highlighted that predictability—rather than the pricing formula itself—was the issue. I convened a rapid-fire planning session with engineering and design, where we sketched out and prioritized a simple “price preview” feature to show customers an up-front estimate before running queries. I personally coded half of the frontend for this component, cut all non-essential extras, and validated the approach with a quick customer check. I also took on customer communications directly, calling stakeholders like Brightside’s CFO to maintain confidence.

4. Results: The impact was immediate and measurable: support tickets about pricing plummeted right after launch, Brightside confirmed their renewal within 24 hours, and the exodus of angry users halted. Customer sentiment visibly shifted, with public praise on social media and a notable example shared by our CEO. The sense of stability returned, and the wider sales team found it easier to re-engage previously hesitant prospects. While further improvements to the pricing model continued, the main crisis—loss of trust—was resolved in under three weeks, securing the threatened revenue and restoring confidence among both customers and the internal team.

5. Takeaways: This experience drove home for me the importance of speed over perfection when trust is at stake. Acting quickly to address users’ core anxieties, rather than getting lost in technical explanations or over-complicating solutions, made all the difference. I also learned to shorten the feedback loop by going straight to customer conversations, rather than over-analyzing data in isolation. The crisis underscored my ability to step outside my usual responsibilities, whether that meant jumping into code or customer support, and drove home just how vital focus and ruthless prioritization can be in a high-stakes startup environment. These lessons have since shaped how I approach urgent product challenges: get close to users fast, cut scope aggressively to deliver value now, and always prioritize rebuilding trust above cleaning up technical debt.

hard_skills.name: Project Documentation
hard_skills.our_take: Core components of 'Project Documentation' include creating and maintaining schedules, status reports, meeting minutes, requirements/specs, onboarding/checklists, and using or producing standardized artifacts that keep stakeholders aligned. The resume does not explicitly state “wrote meeting minutes” or “maintained Jira/Confluence project plans,” but multiple role descriptions reasonably imply hands-on documentation responsibility: 1) As Forward Deployed Engineer, Holden "led the technical implementation for a $2M federal contract" on a six-month timeline and "coordinated with client stakeholders to translate operational requirements into working software" — delivering a federal contract on schedule typically requires maintained requirements, timelines, and delivery artifacts (status reports, technical design docs). 2) He "built internal tools for deployment workflow automation, reducing onboarding time by 25%," which implies he standardized and documented onboarding processes and operational checklists. 3) As Product Manager, he "shipped 3 major features 0→1, defined scope and prioritized the roadmap," "established product analytics framework using Mixpanel and SQL," and "conducted user research with 50+ customers" — these activities commonly produce and rely on product specs, roadmaps, research summaries, and analytics dashboards (document artifacts). 4) Crisis response experience (dynamic pricing rollout) implies producing incident reports/communication artifacts for stakeholders. To strengthen interview/resume credibility for an IDC Junior Data PM role, Holden should add one or two explicit bullets naming the artifacts and tools (e.g., "maintained project schedules and weekly status reports in Jira/Confluence," "authored onboarding playbook and deployment runbooks").

hard_skills.name: Schedule Management
hard_skills.our_take: Alignment to skill: Schedule management requires maintaining/tracking/adjusting timelines, coordinating deadlines across stakeholders, and demonstrating outcomes tied to those actions. This directly implies planning and tracking toward a firm deadline and coordinating cross-functional work to meet it. - As Product Manager at Zo, owned roadmap prioritization, shipped 3 major 0→1 features, reduced feature development cycles by 30%, and led a crisis response to a failed rollout—these activities imply active timeline tradeoffs, prioritization, and rapid re-planning under pressure. - Built internal tools that reduced onboarding time by 25% (Palantir) and established analytics frameworks (Zo) — process improvements that improve schedule predictability. - Participated in incident response at Dropbox, coordinating troubleshooting and communications under time pressure. Evaluation of evidence / transferability: Much of the resume shows direct, practical experience delivering projects against deadlines, prioritizing scope, and coordinating across stakeholders—core parts of schedule management. Proficiency level: The candidate demonstrates proactive schedule-related responsibilities (leading delivery to deadlines, roadmap prioritization, crisis re-planning) consistent with the Intermediate level: actively managing and coordinating timelines and adjusting plans when required. Missing evidence for Advanced/Expert: no clear examples of independently creating contingency schedules, repeatedly balancing multiple complex workstreams, or formalizing schedule management processes/org-wide. Confidence assessment: This is a reasonable inference from their roles (product manager and FDE) where schedule management is typically core; however, some inference was required because specific schedule artifacts or tools are not named. Conclusion: The resume does demonstrate Schedule Management at an Intermediate level (practical application), with a moderate-strong endorsement but would be strengthened by adding explicit schedule artifacts, tools used, and routine schedule-maintenance actions.
hard_skills.why_needed: IDC requires Junior Data PMs to proactively manage and maintain accurate project timelines to keep complex data lake implementation projects on track, minimizing delays, budget overruns, and miscommunications.

Given the direct PM and technical delivery experience plus examples of process improvements and mentoring, Holden meets and in places exceeds the "Intermediate" criteria and shows evidence of the "Advanced" criteria (process improvement, automation, handling dependencies during crisis). Clear judgment: The resume demonstrates deliverables-tracking ability by transferable evidence (leading deliveries on tight timelines, setting/owning roadmap, process automation that improved delivery cadence, and crisis coordination). hard_skills.name: Resource Allocation
hard_skills.our_take:
Alignment to skill: Resource allocation involves assigning people/time/tools, balancing priorities, and tracking who does what to meet schedules. Key evidence looked for: explicit task assignment, scheduling, reallocation decisions, use of allocation tools, or outcomes tied to distribution of resources.

Direct evidence in resume: Holden led the technical delivery of a $2M federal contract at Palantir, coordinating with stakeholders to translate requirements into working software on a six‑month timeline. As Product Manager at Zo Computer, he worked directly with a 4‑person engineering team to define scope and prioritize the roadmap, shipped 3 features from 0→1, implemented analytics (Mixpanel/SQL), reduced feature development cycles by 30%, and led crisis response to a failed rollout. He also mentored four junior engineers and built internal deployment automation that reduced onboarding time by 25%.

Why this supports resource allocation: Leading a technical implementation on a fixed timeline and coordinating scope/priorities with a small engineering team typically requires deciding who works on which tasks, sequencing work, and balancing scope versus capacity — all core resource allocation activities. Mentoring junior engineers and reducing onboarding time implies managing team capacity and improving how resources are ramped. The 30% reduction in development cycles and on‑time delivery of a $2M contract are outcomes consistent with effective allocation and prioritization.

Limitations / inference level: The resume does not explicitly state "assigned tasks," "created schedules/resource plans," "reallocated staff," or specific tools for resource management (e.g., Jira sprint planning, MS Project). Much of the allocation evidence is reasonably inferred from leadership of delivery and roadmap/prioritization responsibilities rather than spelled out.

Proficiency judgement: Given direct leadership of small teams and delivery under time pressure, plus measurable outcomes tied to prioritization and throughput, the background demonstrates practical, hands‑on experience coordinating and distributing work for small projects/teams (i.e., beyond mere awareness but not at the level of cross‑program resource strategy).

What’s missing to strengthen the case: explicit bullets stating task assignment, creation/maintenance of project/resource schedules, budgeting/resource forecasting, tools/processes used for allocation (e.g., sprint planning, resource trackers), or examples of reallocating people or hours to resolve bottlenecks.

Bottom line: The resume demonstrates transferable and partially direct experience in resource allocation suitable for a junior/early‑career PM role, though it would benefit from one or two explicit examples of task assignment or schedule/resource tracking.

hard_skills.our_take: Alignment to skill: Risk Identification requires recognizing, naming, or anticipating potential obstacles and communicating or recording them so they can be mitigated. The resume contains multiple examples where the candidate proactively uncovered problems or put systems in place to surface them. Evidence from resume: - Palantir FDE: deployed across 8 projects and "partnered with product and engineering teams to identify product gaps from field deployments" (identifying issues arising in production/operations); led a $2M federal technical delivery on a 6-month timeline (implies anticipating schedule/technical constraints when coordinating delivery); built internal automation that reduced onboarding time by 25% (a systemic mitigation of onboarding/delivery risk). - Zo Computer (Product Manager): conducted user research with 50+ customers to identify pain points that informed strategy (proactive discovery of adoption/usability risks); established a product analytics framework using Mixpanel and SQL (instrumentation to detect issues/declining metrics early); reduced development cycles via prototyping (addresses risk of long iteration times); led crisis response to a failed dynamic pricing rollout by rapidly diagnosing issues and delivering a real-time solution (demonstrates fast identification and response to emergent risks). - Dropbox internship: participated in incident response during billing downtime (experience identifying/triaging operational incidents). How this maps to the scoring rubric: The resume shows the candidate personally identifying multiple, moderately complex risks (product gaps, customer pain points, rollout failures) and taking mitigation-oriented actions (analytics, prototyping, automation, direct remediation). However, there is no explicit mention of formal risk-management artifacts (risk registers, risk-rating/priority matrices, formal mitigation plans, or escalation protocols) or ownership of organization-level risk processes. Judgment: The profile demonstrates Risk Identification at an intermediate level — the candidate has repeatedly found and acted on risks in product and operational contexts and set up tooling/processes to surface problems, but lacks explicit evidence of systematic/rules-based risk management or formal documentation processes that would indicate an advanced level.
hard_skills.why_needed: IDC needs Risk Identification skills in this role so that junior project managers proactively flag potential threats to data project timelines, deliverables, and budgets, enabling responsive mitigation and escalation to maintain project momentum.

hard_skills.name: Quality Assurance
hard_skills.our_take:
Alignment to skill: Core QA activities include designing/running tests, verifying outputs, catching errors, and improving processes. The resume contains a direct QA example: as a Software Engineering Intern at Dropbox, the candidate "built an automated testing framework for web collaboration features, improving test coverage by 15%," which indicates they designed and implemented tests and measurably improved QA coverage. Additional supporting evidence: participation in high-pressure incident response and hypothesis-driven debugging at Dropbox (troubleshooting and validation under stress); building internal deployment automation tools at Palantir and mentoring on deployment best practices (likely included checks/standards for deployments); and shipping products with reduced support tickets at Zo Computer (suggesting a focus on product quality).

Evaluate evidence for transferable experience: The Dropbox item is a direct QA artifact (test framework + coverage metric). Palantir activities are transferable/adjacent: deployment workflow automation and mentoring on deployment best practices imply attention to consistency and correctness in deployments, which maps to QA process improvement. The product outcomes at Zo (fewer support tickets) are indirect evidence of successful quality control/iteration.

Proficiency and success criteria: The Dropbox bullet demonstrates hands-on creation of test infrastructure and measurable impact (15% coverage improvement) — this aligns with the "novice" to "intermediate" QA criteria (defining/running tests and preventing errors). The Palantir and Zo Computer bullets provide evidence of contributing to QA-related processes and mentorship but lack detail about ownership of QA standards or formal QA governance.

Contextualization: For a Junior Data PM at IDC, QA expectations are at the level of running/assisting QA checks, ensuring documentation and deliverables meet standards, and spotting issues early. The candidate’s direct experience building tests and debugging, plus process automation and mentoring, map well to the responsibilities of supporting QA for data lake projects.

Judgment: The resume demonstrates practical QA experience (direct testing work and process-improvement activities) but lacks explicit QA role titles, detailed tooling/process descriptions (test plans, QA metrics, regression suites, or compliance auditing), and long-term ownership of QA programs. That places the candidate at an early-intermediate level: able to design/run basic QA, assist with QA processes, and contribute to improvements under limited scope/oversight.

If stronger endorsement were required, missing evidence would include explicit QA ownership (e.g., QA lead on projects), details on QA tools/methodologies used for data validation, examples of QA plans or test-case documentation, or metrics showing defect reduction beyond test coverage.
hard_skills.why_needed: IDC requires Junior Data PMs to assist with quality checks, ensuring data lake projects meet documentation, process, and compliance standards, thus minimizing errors and supporting project success in a regulated, high-stakes tech environment.

hard_skills.name: Data Lake Implementation
hard_skills.our_take:
Core components of 'Data Lake Implementation' include understanding data ingestion, raw/unstructured storage, metadata/cataloging, ETL/ELT pipelines, governance/security, and coordinating technical and non-technical stakeholders during rollout. The resume contains no explicit mention of "data lake" or common data-lake tooling (e.g., S3, HDFS, Databricks, Snowflake, Glue). However, it does show relevant, transferable experience: multiple Palantir FDE engagements where the candidate "built custom data integration and analysis solutions for mission-critical operations" and "led technical implementation" for a $2M federal contract, which reasonably implies hands-on work with integrating varied data sources and deploying data-centric systems. The candidate also established product analytics using SQL and Mixpanel at Zo Computer, which indicates familiarity with data tracking, ingestion of event data, and analytics pipelines. Together these items suggest practical exposure to data-platform projects and the ability to work with engineers and stakeholders on data-related implementations.

Gaps: there is no explicit evidence of implementing or managing a data lake, running ingestion pipelines, handling large-scale raw data storage, or using cloud data-lake technologies. The resume does not mention data governance, cataloging, ETL tools, or specific cloud platforms. For IDC's Junior Data PM role (entry-level supporting data lake implementations), the candidate shows a solid transferable foundation but lacks direct, explicit data-lake experience.

Judgment: The profile supports a novice-level endorsement — the candidate has relevant, hands-on data-integration and analytics experience that should translate well to a junior support role on data lake projects, but the resume should be updated to call out any direct exposure to data-lake concepts, ingestion, pipelines, or cloud storage to strengthen the case.
hard_skills.why_needed: IDC requires candidates with foundational knowledge of data lakes to ensure they can understand discussions, support documentation, and communicate effectively in projects centered on data lake implementation.

Transferable experience and context: The candidate’s roles required repeated translation between technical teams, product/design, customers, and government stakeholders — situations that mirror the Junior Data PM need to communicate between technical (data engineers) and non-technical stakeholders. Judgment: The resume demonstrates Effective Communication at an Advanced level: proactive, cross-audience communication that produced systemic outcomes and resolved high-stakes issues. These are minor for entry-level PM hiring managers but would be useful to call out.

Judgment: The resume demonstrates solid, largely direct evidence of problem-solving skills at an Advanced (applied theory) level — the candidate has repeatedly identified complex issues, coordinated cross-functional solutions, and delivered measurable impact.

Recommended short improvement for interview/resume: Add 1–2 brief STAR bullets that explicitly call out the obstacle, options considered, why a particular approach was chosen, and the measured outcome (and name any PM methodologies or tools used) to make the problem-solving thread even clearer to PM-focused reviewers.
soft_skills.why_needed: IDC needs strong problem-solving skills in Junior Data PMs to ensure issues in documentation, schedules, communication, or data workflows are proactively identified and resolved, minimizing project delays and supporting technical and business goals.

soft_skills.name: Issue Escalation
soft_skills.our_take: Alignment to skill: Issue Escalation requires recognizing problems beyond one’s authority and deliberately communicating them upward or to the right stakeholders, with judgment about timing and appropriate channels. Relevant resume evidence: (1) Zo Computer — “led the crisis response to a failed dynamic pricing rollout, rapidly diagnosing issues, engaging directly with affected key customers, and delivering a real-time solution that prevented major revenue loss and restored client trust.” This shows problem recognition, rapid engagement of stakeholders, and outcome-driven action in a high-stakes incident. (2) Dropbox — “actively participated in high-pressure incident response, coordinating troubleshooting and communication during a major billing downtime,” which implies coordination and escalation-style communication under pressure. (3) Palantir FDE — frequent collaboration with client stakeholders on mission-critical operations, leading technical delivery for federal contracts, and identifying product gaps that were escalated to product/engineering and resulted in company-wide improvements. Evaluation vs. success criteria: The resume demonstrates multiple situations where the candidate recognized operationally significant problems, coordinated communication with stakeholders, and either led or participated in incident responses that produced clear outcomes. However, the resume does not explicitly state formal escalation actions (e.g., “escalated to senior leadership,” “followed escalation protocol X,” or “raised issue to project sponsor”) or provide details on triggers, timelines, or handoffs. Judgment about escalation necessity and the procedural side are largely inferred from the crisis/incident language and the candidate’s leadership roles. Proficiency and outcome: The cited incidents resulted in positive outcomes (prevented revenue loss, restored client trust, improved product/platform), indicating effective escalation-style behavior and follow-through. Where it falls short of higher levels: there’s no explicit evidence of designing escalation frameworks, training others on escalation, or documenting escalation protocols. Conclusion: The resume provides reasonable, direct evidence of issue escalation behavior at a practical level (identifying problems, coordinating stakeholders, and getting resolution), but lacks explicit procedural detail about formal escalation steps or thresholds. To strengthen the case, the candidate should add a concise STAR bullet that names who they escalated issues to, the decision point/triggers, and the immediate result of the escalation.
soft_skills.why_needed: IDC needs entry-level Data PMs who can reliably recognize and raise issues to project leadership or sponsors before they impact project delivery, ensuring minor risks do not become major failures in high-stakes, multi-stakeholder technology projects.

soft_skills.name: Empathy
soft_skills.our_take: The resume provides multiple concrete, work-related examples that show the candidate recognizes others' perspectives and acts on them. Key evidence: (1) Conducted user research with 50+ customers whose feedback directly shaped product strategy and drove a 40% DAU increase — this implies active listening, understanding customer needs, and translating them into product decisions. (2) Led crisis response for a failed dynamic pricing rollout, engaging directly with affected key customers and delivering a real-time solution that restored trust and prevented revenue loss — this demonstrates responsiveness to stakeholder concerns and adapting communication/solutions under pressure. (3) Mentored four junior FDEs and created internal onboarding tools that reduced new-client onboarding time by 25% — showing awareness of teammates' learning needs and removing friction for others. (4) Palantir role translating operational requirements into working software and collaborating with stakeholders indicates perspective-taking between technical and non-technical users. Supplementary evidence: Dropbox intern incident response and collaboration with product/design also suggest calm, empathic team communication during high-pressure events. These concrete examples meet the resume criteria for applying empathy to influence outcomes (adapting to customers, resolving crises, mentoring). Missing/limited: the resume rarely uses explicit language about emotional support, mediation, or deliberate adaptation of communication style across diverse personalities — most empathy evidence is inferred from outcomes and activities rather than spelled-out interpersonal behaviors. Overall, the experiences align with the "Intermediate" empathy criteria (applying empathy to influence project or team outcomes), but because some inference was necessary rather than direct statements, I rate strength as 7/10 with a confidence of 7/10.
soft_skills.why_needed: Empathy is crucial in the Junior Data PM role at IDC to foster clear communication, anticipate stakeholder needs, and support positive collaboration among technical and non-technical team members.
